para.text: Improper management of credentials, such as usernames and passwords, may compromise system security. In particular, storing passwords in plaintext or hard-coding passwords directly into application code are design issues that cannot be easily remedied. Not only does embedding a password allow all of the project's developers to view the password, it also makes fixing the problem extremely difficult. Once the code is in production, the password cannot be changed without patching the software. If a hard-coded password is compromised in a commercial product, all deployed instances may be vulnerable to attack, putting customers at risk.

One variation on hard-coding plaintext passwords is to hard-code a constant string which is the result of a cryptographic one-way hash. For example, instead of storing the word "secret," the application stores an MD5 hash of the word. This is a common mechanism for obscuring hard-coded passwords from casual viewing but does not significantly reduce risk. However, using cryptographic hashes for data stored outside the application code can be an effective practice.

Avoid storing passwords in easily accessible locations, and never store any type of sensitive data in plaintext. Avoid using hard-coded usernames, passwords, or hash constants whenever possible, particularly in relation to security-critical components. Store passwords out-of-band from the application code. Follow best practices for protecting credentials stored in alternate locations such as configuration or properties files.

text.text: A method uses a hard-coded password that may compromise system security in a way that cannot be easily remedied. The use of a hard-coded password significantly increases the possibility that the account being protected will be compromised. Moreover, the password cannot be changed without patching the software. If a hard-coded password is compromised in a commercial product, all deployed instances may be vulnerable to attack.

flaw.description:
This variable assignment uses a hard-coded password that may compromise system security in a way that cannot be easily remedied. The use of a hard-coded password significantly increases the possibility that the account being protected will be compromised. Moreover, the password cannot be changed without patching the software. If a hard-coded password is compromised in a commercial product, all deployed instances may be vulnerable to attack. In some cases, this finding may indicate a reference to a password (e.g. the name of a key in a properties file) rather than an actual password. GetSecretFromVault secretName
Store passwords out-of-band from the application code. Follow best practices for protecting credentials stored in locations such as configuration or properties files. An HSM may be appropriate for particularly sensitive credentials.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/259.html)

mitigation.description: Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : These calls are not using hardcoded passwords. Passwords and secrets are retrieved from our Azure Keyvault and passed to authentication methods and internal service calls. Remaining Risk : none Verification : code reviewed

Proposal approved because the line of code identified as vulnerable by Veracode does not contain any hardcoded passwords. Instead, it uses placeholder variables that retrieve data from the configuration file. Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design.

para.text: An information leak is the intentional or unintentional disclosure of information that is either regarded as sensitive within the product's own functionality or provides information about the product or its environment that could be useful in an attack. Information leakage issues are commonly overlooked because they cannot be used to directly exploit the application. However, information leaks should be viewed as building blocks that an attacker uses to carry out other, more complicated attacks.

There are many different types of problems that involve information leaks, with severities that can range widely depending on the type of information leaked and the context of the information with respect to the application. Common sources of information leakage include, but are not limited to:

bulletitem.text: Log files or backup files in web-accessible directories
Transmission of uninitialized memory containing sensitive data

para.text: Configure applications and servers to return generic error messages and to suppress stack traces from being displayed to end users. Ensure that errors generated by the application do not provide insight into specific backend issues.

Remove all backup files, binary archives, alternate versions of files, and test files from web-accessible directories of production servers. The only files that should be present in the application's web document root are files required by the application. Ensure that deployment procedures include the removal of these file types by an administrator. Keep web and application servers fully patched to minimize exposure to publicly-disclosed information leakage vulnerabilities.

text.text: The web server receives a URL or similar request from an upstream component and retrieves the contents of this URL, but it does not sufficiently ensure that the request is being sent to the expected destination.

flaw.description:
The application does not sufficiently protect against server-side request forgery (SSRF) attacks. In the call to fetch() untrusted input is used in the creation of a web-request which possibly allows an attacker to perform unauthorized requests to internal or external systems. An attacker can use this to interact with internal services or disclose information or even local server files.
Validate the untrusted data used to create the web-request and, additionally, validate the end-result URL before performing the request.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/918.html) OWASP (https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Server_Side_Request_Forgery)

mitigation.description: Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : These calls are internal or status response calls from our own API endpoints. Remaining Risk : none Verification : Code reviewed

Proposal rejected because the proposed mitigation does not address the flaw. Veracode identify that untrusted input is used to create the webrequest. The application needs to validate the untrusted data used to create the web-request and, additionally, validate the end-result URL ip-address/domain-name before performing the request or get the base URL from the configuration/environment to avoid an SSRF.

Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : This flag is on a status query URI call that is being used within a private method in a front end service. This URL is returned a standard C# orchestration function URL to retrieve status information for longer running requests without waiting within the application. Remaining Risk : None. This is a private method accepting an orchestration function status retrieval URL Verification : Code reviewed

Proposal approved because the URL is obtained from the response of the system’s own backend service call. The backend request uses a configured base URL (${AppSettings.env.azureFunctionURL}), which is controlled by the application configuration and not influenced by user input. The response, including StatusQueryGetUri, is generated by the trusted backend service and consumed internally. As a result, the URL is not user‑controlled and does not present a security risk.

flaw.description:
The ASP.NET MVC Core Application does not sufficiently protect against server-side request forgery (SSRF) attacks. In the call to system_net_http_dll.System.Net.Http.HttpClient.PostAsync() untrusted input is used in the creation of a web-request which possibly allows an attacker to perform unauthorized requests to internal or external systems. An attacker can use this to interact with internal services or disclose information or even local server files.
Validate the untrusted data used to create the web-request and, additionally, validate the end-result URL before performing the request.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/918.html) OWASP (https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Server_Side_Request_Forgery)

para.text: The acronym CRLF stands for "Carriage Return, Line Feed" and refers to the sequence of characters used to denote the end of a line of text. CRLF injection vulnerabilities occur when data enters an application from an untrusted source and is not properly validated before being used. For example, if an attacker is able to inject a CRLF into a log file, he could append falsified log entries, thereby misleading administrators or cover traces of the attack. If an attacker is able to inject CRLFs into an HTTP response header, he can use this ability to carry out other attacks such as cache poisoning. CRLF vulnerabilities primarily affect data integrity.

Apply robust input filtering for all user-supplied data, using centralized data validation routines when possible. Use output filters to sanitize all output derived from user-supplied input, replacing non-alphanumeric characters with their HTML entity equivalents.

text.text: A function call could result in a log forging attack. Writing untrusted data into a log file allows an attacker to forge log entries or inject malicious content into log files. Corrupted log files can be used to cover an attacker's tracks or as a delivery mechanism for an attack on a log viewing or processing utility. For example, if a web administrator uses a browser-based utility to review logs, a cross-site scripting attack might be possible.

flaw.description:
This call to microsoft_extensions_logging_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.LoggerExtensions.LogInformation() could result in a log forging attack. Writing untrusted data into a log file allows an attacker to forge log entries or inject malicious content into log files. Corrupted log files can be used to cover an attacker's tracks or as a delivery mechanism for an attack on a log viewing or processing utility. For example, if a web administrator uses a browser-based utility to review logs, a cross-site scripting attack might be possible. The second argument to LogInformation() contains tainted data from the variable Concat(). The tainted data originated from earlier calls to microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Query, and microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Headers.
Avoid directly embedding user input in log files when possible. Sanitize untrusted data used to construct log entries by using a safe logging mechanism such as the OWASP ESAPI Logger, which will automatically remove unexpected carriage returns and line feeds and can be configured to use HTML entity encoding for non-alphanumeric data. Alternatively, some of the XSS escaping functions from the OWASP Java Encoder project will also sanitize CRLF sequences. Only create a custom blocklist when absolutely necessary. Always validate untrusted input to ensure that it conforms to the expected format, using centralized data validation routines when possible.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html) OWASP (https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Log_Injection) Supported Cleansers (https://docs.veracode.com/r/review_cleansers?tocId=nYnZqAenFFZmB75MQrZwuA)

mitigation.description: Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : Internal logging information passed from authenticated endpoints with previously parsed and verified input parameters Remaining Risk : none Verification : code reviewed

Proposal rejected because the proposed mitigation does not address the flaw. CWE-117 refers to a security weakness where an application fails to properly sanitize user-input data before writing it to log files. This can allow attackers to inject malicious content or forge log entries. The issue is related to Carriage Return and Line Feed (CRLF) characters, where a custom or built-in function needs to replace \r and \n. Please check for more reference, https://docs.veracode.com/r/Supported_NET_Cleansing_Function

mitigation.description: Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : Verified and parsed internal data being logged to our application insights Remaining Risk : none Verification : code reviewed

Proposal rejected because the proposed mitigation does not address the flaw. CWE-117 refers to a security weakness where an application fails to properly sanitize user-input data before writing it to log files. This can allow attackers to inject malicious content or forge log entries. The issue is related to Carriage Return and Line Feed (CRLF) characters, where a custom or built-in function needs to replace \r and \n. Please check for more reference, https://docs.veracode.com/r/Supported_NET_Cleansing_Function

flaw.description:
This call to microsoft_extensions_logging_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.LoggerExtensions.LogInformation() could result in a log forging attack. Writing untrusted data into a log file allows an attacker to forge log entries or inject malicious content into log files. Corrupted log files can be used to cover an attacker's tracks or as a delivery mechanism for an attack on a log viewing or processing utility. For example, if a web administrator uses a browser-based utility to review logs, a cross-site scripting attack might be possible. The second argument to LogInformation() contains tainted data from the variable ToStringAndClear(). The tainted data originated from earlier calls to microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Query, and microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Headers.
Avoid directly embedding user input in log files when possible. Sanitize untrusted data used to construct log entries by using a safe logging mechanism such as the OWASP ESAPI Logger, which will automatically remove unexpected carriage returns and line feeds and can be configured to use HTML entity encoding for non-alphanumeric data. Alternatively, some of the XSS escaping functions from the OWASP Java Encoder project will also sanitize CRLF sequences. Only create a custom blocklist when absolutely necessary. Always validate untrusted input to ensure that it conforms to the expected format, using centralized data validation routines when possible.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html) OWASP (https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Log_Injection) Supported Cleansers (https://docs.veracode.com/r/review_cleansers?tocId=nYnZqAenFFZmB75MQrZwuA)

flaw.description:
This call to microsoft_extensions_logging_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.LoggerExtensions.LogWarning() could result in a log forging attack. Writing untrusted data into a log file allows an attacker to forge log entries or inject malicious content into log files. Corrupted log files can be used to cover an attacker's tracks or as a delivery mechanism for an attack on a log viewing or processing utility. For example, if a web administrator uses a browser-based utility to review logs, a cross-site scripting attack might be possible. The second argument to LogWarning() contains tainted data from the variable Concat(). The tainted data originated from earlier calls to microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Query, and microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Headers.

CWE-117 refers to a security weakness where an application fails to properly sanitize user-input data before writing it to log files. This can allow attackers to inject malicious content or forge log entries. The issue is related to Carriage Return and Line Feed (CRLF) characters, where a custom or built-in function needs to replace \r and \n. Please check for more reference, https://docs.veracode.com/r/Supported_NET_Cleansing_Function

flaw.description:
This call to microsoft_extensions_logging_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.LoggerExtensions.LogInformation() could result in a log forging attack. Writing untrusted data into a log file allows an attacker to forge log entries or inject malicious content into log files. Corrupted log files can be used to cover an attacker's tracks or as a delivery mechanism for an attack on a log viewing or processing utility. For example, if a web administrator uses a browser-based utility to review logs, a cross-site scripting attack might be possible. The second argument to LogInformation() contains tainted data from the variable ToStringAndClear(). The tainted data originated from earlier calls to microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Query, and microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Headers.
Avoid directly embedding user input in log files when possible. Sanitize untrusted data used to construct log entries by using a safe logging mechanism such as the OWASP ESAPI Logger, which will automatically remove unexpected carriage returns and line feeds and can be configured to use HTML entity encoding for non-alphanumeric data. Alternatively, some of the XSS escaping functions from the OWASP Java Encoder project will also sanitize CRLF sequences. Only create a custom blocklist when absolutely necessary. Always validate untrusted input to ensure that it conforms to the expected format, using centralized data validation routines when possible.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html) OWASP (https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Log_Injection) Supported Cleansers (https://docs.veracode.com/r/review_cleansers?tocId=nYnZqAenFFZmB75MQrZwuA)

The issue is related to Carriage Return and Line Feed (CRLF) characters, where a custom or built-in function needs to replace \r and \n. Please check for more reference, https://docs.veracode.com/r/Supported_NET_Cleansing_Function

flaw.description:
This call to microsoft_extensions_logging_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.LoggerExtensions.LogInformation() could result in a log forging attack. Writing untrusted data into a log file allows an attacker to forge log entries or inject malicious content into log files. Corrupted log files can be used to cover an attacker's tracks or as a delivery mechanism for an attack on a log viewing or processing utility. For example, if a web administrator uses a browser-based utility to review logs, a cross-site scripting attack might be possible. The second argument to LogInformation() contains tainted data from the variable ToStringAndClear(). The tainted data originated from earlier calls to microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Query, and microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Headers.
Avoid directly embedding user input in log files when possible. Sanitize untrusted data used to construct log entries by using a safe logging mechanism such as the OWASP ESAPI Logger, which will automatically remove unexpected carriage returns and line feeds and can be configured to use HTML entity encoding for non-alphanumeric data. Alternatively, some of the XSS escaping functions from the OWASP Java Encoder project will also sanitize CRLF sequences. Only create a custom blocklist when absolutely necessary. Always validate untrusted input to ensure that it conforms to the expected format, using centralized data validation routines when possible.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html) OWASP (https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Log_Injection) Supported Cleansers (https://docs.veracode.com/r/review_cleansers?tocId=nYnZqAenFFZmB75MQrZwuA)

CWE-117 refers to a security weakness where an application fails to properly sanitize user-input data before writing it to log files. This can allow attackers to inject malicious content or forge log entries. The issue is related to Carriage Return and Line Feed (CRLF) characters, where a custom or built-in function needs to replace \r and \n. Please check for more reference, https://docs.veracode.com/r/Supported_NET_Cleansing_Function

flaw.description:
This call to microsoft_extensions_logging_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.LoggerExtensions.LogInformation() could result in a log forging attack. Writing untrusted data into a log file allows an attacker to forge log entries or inject malicious content into log files. Corrupted log files can be used to cover an attacker's tracks or as a delivery mechanism for an attack on a log viewing or processing utility. For example, if a web administrator uses a browser-based utility to review logs, a cross-site scripting attack might be possible. The second argument to LogInformation() contains tainted data from the variable ToStringAndClear(). The tainted data originated from earlier calls to microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Query, and microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Headers.
Avoid directly embedding user input in log files when possible. Sanitize untrusted data used to construct log entries by using a safe logging mechanism such as the OWASP ESAPI Logger, which will automatically remove unexpected carriage returns and line feeds and can be configured to use HTML entity encoding for non-alphanumeric data. Alternatively, some of the XSS escaping functions from the OWASP Java Encoder project will also sanitize CRLF sequences. Only create a custom blocklist when absolutely necessary. Always validate untrusted input to ensure that it conforms to the expected format, using centralized data validation routines when possible.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html) OWASP (https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Log_Injection) Supported Cleansers (https://docs.veracode.com/r/review_cleansers?tocId=nYnZqAenFFZmB75MQrZwuA)

mitigation.description: Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : Verified and parsed internal data being logged to our application insights Remaining Risk : none Verification : code reviewed

Proposal rejected because the proposed mitigation does not address the flaw. CWE-117 refers to a security weakness where an application fails to properly sanitize user-input data before writing it to log files. This can allow attackers to inject malicious content or forge log entries. The issue is related to Carriage Return and Line Feed (CRLF) characters, where a custom or built-in function needs to replace \r and \n. Please check for more reference, https://docs.veracode.com/r/Supported_NET_Cleansing_Function

flaw.description:
This call to microsoft_extensions_logging_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.LoggerExtensions.LogInformation() could result in a log forging attack. Writing untrusted data into a log file allows an attacker to forge log entries or inject malicious content into log files. Corrupted log files can be used to cover an attacker's tracks or as a delivery mechanism for an attack on a log viewing or processing utility. For example, if a web administrator uses a browser-based utility to review logs, a cross-site scripting attack might be possible. The second argument to LogInformation() contains tainted data from the variable ToStringAndClear(). The tainted data originated from earlier calls to microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Query, and microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Headers.
Avoid directly embedding user input in log files when possible. Sanitize untrusted data used to construct log entries by using a safe logging mechanism such as the OWASP ESAPI Logger, which will automatically remove unexpected carriage returns and line feeds and can be configured to use HTML entity encoding for non-alphanumeric data. Alternatively, some of the XSS escaping functions from the OWASP Java Encoder project will also sanitize CRLF sequences. Only create a custom blocklist when absolutely necessary. Always validate untrusted input to ensure that it conforms to the expected format, using centralized data validation routines when possible.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html) OWASP (https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Log_Injection) Supported Cleansers (https://docs.veracode.com/r/review_cleansers?tocId=nYnZqAenFFZmB75MQrZwuA)

mitigation.description: Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : Verified and parsed internal data being logged to our application insights Remaining Risk : none Verification : code reviewed

Proposal rejected because the proposed mitigation does not address the flaw. CWE-117 refers to a security weakness where an application fails to properly sanitize user-input data before writing it to log files. This can allow attackers to inject malicious content or forge log entries. The issue is related to Carriage Return and Line Feed (CRLF) characters, where a custom or built-in function needs to replace \r and \n. Please check for more reference, https://docs.veracode.com/r/Supported_NET_Cleansing_Function

flaw.description:
This call to microsoft_extensions_logging_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.LoggerExtensions.LogWarning() could result in a log forging attack. Writing untrusted data into a log file allows an attacker to forge log entries or inject malicious content into log files. Corrupted log files can be used to cover an attacker's tracks or as a delivery mechanism for an attack on a log viewing or processing utility. For example, if a web administrator uses a browser-based utility to review logs, a cross-site scripting attack might be possible. The second argument to LogWarning() contains tainted data from the variable ToStringAndClear(). The tainted data originated from earlier calls to microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Query, and microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Headers.

For example, if a web administrator uses a browser-based utility to review logs, a cross-site scripting attack might be possible. The second argument to LogInformation() contains tainted data from the variable ToStringAndClear(). The tainted data originated from earlier calls to microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Query, and microsoft_aspnetcore_http_abstractions_dll.Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.HttpRequest.get_Headers.
Avoid directly embedding user input in log files when possible. Sanitize untrusted data used to construct log entries by using a safe logging mechanism such as the OWASP ESAPI Logger, which will automatically remove unexpected carriage returns and line feeds and can be configured to use HTML entity encoding for non-alphanumeric data. Alternatively, some of the XSS escaping functions from the OWASP Java Encoder project will also sanitize CRLF sequences. Only create a custom blocklist when absolutely necessary. Always validate untrusted input to ensure that it conforms to the expected format, using centralized data validation routines when possible.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html) OWASP (https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Log_Injection) Supported Cleansers (https://docs.veracode.com/r/review_cleansers?tocId=nYnZqAenFFZmB75MQrZwuA)

para.text: An information leak is the intentional or unintentional disclosure of information that is either regarded as sensitive within the product's own functionality or provides information about the product or its environment that could be useful in an attack. Information leakage issues are commonly overlooked because they cannot be used to directly exploit the application. However, information leaks should be viewed as building blocks that an attacker uses to carry out other, more complicated attacks.

There are many different types of problems that involve information leaks, with severities that can range widely depending on the type of information leaked and the context of the information with respect to the application. Common sources of information leakage include, but are not limited to:

bulletitem.text: Log files or backup files in web-accessible directories
Transmission of uninitialized memory containing sensitive data

para.text: Configure applications and servers to return generic error messages and to suppress stack traces from being displayed to end users. Ensure that errors generated by the application do not provide insight into specific backend issues.

Remove all backup files, binary archives, alternate versions of files, and test files from web-accessible directories of production servers. The only files that should be present in the application's web document root are files required by the application. Ensure that deployment procedures include the removal of these file types by an administrator. Keep web and application servers fully patched to minimize exposure to publicly-disclosed information leakage vulnerabilities.

cwe.cwename: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

text.text: The software generates an error message that includes sensitive information about its environment, users, or associated data. The sensitive information may be valuable information on its own (such as a password), or it may be useful for launching other, more deadly attacks. If an attack fails, an attacker may use error information provided by the server to launch another more focused attack. For example, file locations disclosed by an exception stack trace may be leveraged by an attacker to exploit a path traversal issue elsewhere in the application.

flaw.categoryname: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
flaw.description:
The application calls the system_runtime_dll.System.IO.TextWriter.WriteLine() function, which may expose information about the application logic or other details such as the names and versions of the application container and associated components. This information can be useful in executing other attacks and can also enable the attacker to target known vulnerabilities in application components. The first argument to WriteLine() contains data from an error message (possibly containing untrusted data) from the variable Concat(). The data from an error message (possibly containing untrusted data) originated from an earlier call to system_runtime_dll.System.Exception.ToString.
Ensure that error codes or other messages returned to end users are not overly verbose. Sanitize all messages of any sensitive information that is not absolutely necessary.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/209.html)

mitigation.description: Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : Azure worker functions logging Remaining Risk : none Verification : code reviewed

Proposal rejected because the proposed mitigation does not address the flaw. Please explain what type of message the application returns when an exception occurs. Ensure that no exception or error messages containing potentially sensitive or verbose information are shown to the user. Always display static and controlled messages. Implement default error pages or messages that do not leak any internal details.

cwe.cwename: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data

text.text: Sensitive information may be exposed as a result of outbound network connections made by the application.

flaw.categoryname: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
flaw.description:
The application calls the system_net_http_dll.System.Net.Http.HttpClient.PostAsync() function, which will result in data being transferred out of the application (via the network or another medium). This data contains sensitive information. PostAsync() was called on the client object, which contains potentially sensitive data. The potentially sensitive data originated from an earlier call to system_configuration_dll.System.Configuration.ConfigurationManager.get_AppSettings.
Ensure that the transfer of the sensitive data is intended and that it does not violate application security policy. This flaw is categorized as low severity because it only impacts confidentiality, not integrity or availability. However, in the context of a mobile application, the significance of an information leak may be much greater, especially if misaligned with user expectations or data privacy policies.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/201.html) OWASP Security Misconfiguration (https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/) OWASP Cryptographic Failures (https://owasp.org/Top10/A02_2021-Cryptographic_Failures/)

mitigation.description: Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : Internal Avepoint integration calls that hit our own endpoints. We have control over all data in and out of this integration service. Remaining Risk : none Verification : code reviewed

Proposal rejected because more information is required. The Veracode identified that the application is sending sensitive data. Please describe what data is being sent in the line of code Veracode has identified the issue. If data is intentionally sent, confirm whether it is sensitive data and, if so, include encryption of the data in transit (e.g.., using TLS1.2/HTTPS)

Please describe what data is being sent in the line of code Veracode has identified the issue. If data is intentionally sent, confirm whether it is sensitive data and, if so, include encryption of the data in transit (e.g.., using TLS1.2/HTTPS)

flaw.categoryname: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
flaw.description:
The application calls the system_net_http_dll.System.Net.Http.HttpClient.PostAsync() function, which will result in data being transferred out of the application (via the network or another medium). This data contains sensitive information. The first argument to PostAsync() contains potentially sensitive data from the variable reqUri. The potentially sensitive data originated from an earlier call to system_configuration_dll.System.Configuration.ConfigurationManager.get_AppSettings.
Ensure that the transfer of the sensitive data is intended and that it does not violate application security policy. This flaw is categorized as low severity because it only impacts confidentiality, not integrity or availability. However, in the context of a mobile application, the significance of an information leak may be much greater, especially if misaligned with user expectations or data privacy policies.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/201.html) OWASP Security Misconfiguration (https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/) OWASP Cryptographic Failures (https://owasp.org/Top10/A02_2021-Cryptographic_Failures/)

cwe.cwename: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data

text.text: Sensitive information may be exposed as a result of outbound network connections made by the application.

flaw.categoryname: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
flaw.description:
The application calls the system_net_http_dll.System.Net.Http.HttpClient.PostAsync() function, which will result in data being transferred out of the application (via the network or another medium). This data contains sensitive information. PostAsync() was called on the httpClient object, which contains potentially sensitive data. The potentially sensitive data originated from an earlier call to system_runtime_dll.System.Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable.
Ensure that the transfer of the sensitive data is intended and that it does not violate application security policy. This flaw is categorized as low severity because it only impacts confidentiality, not integrity or availability. However, in the context of a mobile application, the significance of an information leak may be much greater, especially if misaligned with user expectations or data privacy policies.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/201.html) OWASP Security Misconfiguration (https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/) OWASP Cryptographic Failures (https://owasp.org/Top10/A02_2021-Cryptographic_Failures/)

mitigation.description: Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : Internal data which is reviewed and logged/sent through our managed system. Data is not sensitive Remaining Risk : none Verification : code reviewed
Proposal approved because the application is not sending any sensitive information and it has control over all the data/parameters it passes over for internal communication.

flaw.categoryname: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
flaw.description:
The application calls the system_net_http_dll.System.Net.Http.HttpClient.PostAsync() function, which will result in data being transferred out of the application (via the network or another medium). This data contains sensitive information. The second argument to PostAsync() contains potentially sensitive data from the variable \5__4. The potentially sensitive data originated from an earlier call to system_runtime_dll.System.Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable.
Ensure that the transfer of the sensitive data is intended and that it does not violate application security policy. This flaw is categorized as low severity because it only impacts confidentiality, not integrity or availability. However, in the context of a mobile application, the significance of an information leak may be much greater, especially if misaligned with user expectations or data privacy policies.

flaw.categoryname: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
flaw.description:
The application calls the system_net_http_dll.System.Net.Http.HttpClient.PostAsync() function, which will result in data being transferred out of the application (via the network or another medium). This data contains sensitive information. The second argument to PostAsync() contains potentially sensitive data from the variable \5__4. The potentially sensitive data originated from an earlier call to system_runtime_dll.System.Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable.
Ensure that the transfer of the sensitive data is intended and that it does not violate application security policy. This flaw is categorized as low severity because it only impacts confidentiality, not integrity or availability. However, in the context of a mobile application, the significance of an information leak may be much greater, especially if misaligned with user expectations or data privacy policies.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/201.html) OWASP Security Misconfiguration (https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/) OWASP Cryptographic Failures (https://owasp.org/Top10/A02_2021-Cryptographic_Failures/)

mitigation.description: Technique : M1 : Establish and maintain control over all of your inputs Specifics : Internal data which is reviewed and logged/sent through our managed system. Data is not sensitive Remaining Risk : none Verification : code reviewed
Proposal approved because the application is not sending any sensitive information and it has control over all the data/parameters it passes over for internal communication.

flaw.categoryname: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
flaw.description:
The application calls the system_net_http_dll.System.Net.Http.HttpClient.DeleteAsync() function, which will result in data being transferred out of the application (via the network or another medium). This data contains sensitive information. DeleteAsync() was called on the httpClient object, which contains potentially sensitive data. The potentially sensitive data originated from an earlier call to system_runtime_dll.System.Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable.
Ensure that the transfer of the sensitive data is intended and that it does not violate application security policy. This flaw is categorized as low severity because it only impacts confidentiality, not integrity or availability. However, in the context of a mobile application, the significance of an information leak may be much greater, especially if misaligned with user expectations or data privacy policies.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/201.html) OWASP Security Misconfiguration (https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/) OWASP Cryptographic Failures (https://owasp.org/Top10/A02_2021-Cryptographic_Failures/)

Proposal approved because the application is not sending any sensitive information and it has control over all the data/parameters it passes over for internal communication.

flaw.categoryname: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
flaw.description:
The application calls the system_net_http_dll.System.Net.Http.HttpClient.PostAsync() function, which will result in data being transferred out of the application (via the network or another medium). This data contains sensitive information. PostAsync() was called on the client object, which contains potentially sensitive data. The potentially sensitive data originated from an earlier call to system_configuration_dll.System.Configuration.ConfigurationManager.get_AppSettings.
Ensure that the transfer of the sensitive data is intended and that it does not violate application security policy. This flaw is categorized as low severity because it only impacts confidentiality, not integrity or availability. However, in the context of a mobile application, the significance of an information leak may be much greater, especially if misaligned with user expectations or data privacy policies.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/201.html) OWASP Security Misconfiguration (https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/) OWASP Cryptographic Failures (https://owasp.org/Top10/A02_2021-Cryptographic_Failures/)
flaw.functionprototype: void WorkspacePostChangeAction(string, string, string, string, string, string)

mitigation.description: Technique : GP2 : Integrate security into the entire software development lifecycle Specifics : This calls our internal logic app when an Update Status (close, reopen, legal hold, release legal hold) is processed for a non-Teams site. We pass in the site URL, the requester of the process, the status, the Avepoint request ID, and the Avepoint request number. Remaining Risk : None Verification : Manually verified
Approved because sensitive information is passed as parameter to fetch details only,intended and stored in secure medium. Hence satisfies the mitigate by design..

flaw.categoryname: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
flaw.description:
The application calls the system_net_http_dll.System.Net.Http.HttpClient.PostAsync() function, which will result in data being transferred out of the application (via the network or another medium). This data contains sensitive information. The second argument to PostAsync() contains potentially sensitive data from the variable requestContent. The potentially sensitive data originated from an earlier call to system_configuration_dll.System.Configuration.ConfigurationManager.get_AppSettings.
Ensure that the transfer of the sensitive data is intended and that it does not violate application security policy. This flaw is categorized as low severity because it only impacts confidentiality, not integrity or availability. However, in the context of a mobile application, the significance of an information leak may be much greater, especially if misaligned with user expectations or data privacy policies.

flaw.categoryname: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
flaw.description:
The application calls the system_net_http_dll.System.Net.Http.HttpClient.PostAsync() function, which will result in data being transferred out of the application (via the network or another medium). This data contains sensitive information. The first argument to PostAsync() contains potentially sensitive data from the variable \5__2. The potentially sensitive data originated from an earlier call to system_runtime_dll.System.Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable.
Ensure that the transfer of the sensitive data is intended and that it does not violate application security policy. This flaw is categorized as low severity because it only impacts confidentiality, not integrity or availability. However, in the context of a mobile application, the significance of an information leak may be much greater, especially if misaligned with user expectations or data privacy policies.

mitigation.description: Technique : GP2 : Integrate security into the entire software development lifecycle Specifics : The URL that is requested here is built using data that is stored in our Azure Function configuration. There is no user input. Remaining Risk : None Verification : Manually verified

Rejected because we need more information on this. Kindly specify if the call or request is transmitted over HTTPS, or if TLS 1.2 or above implemented in the network level (If any communication is taking place over the network).

Technique : GP2 : Integrate security into the entire software development lifecycle Specifics : These calls all go through our Azure Functions which are all set to allow only HTTPS traffic and minimum TLS version 1.2 which follows PwC Azure Policies. Remaining Risk : None Verification : Manually verified

Technique : GP2 : Integrate security into the entire software development lifecycle Specifics : Also, there is no sensitive data in any of these endpoints. Remaining Risk : None Verification : Manually verified
Approved because the communication is over HTTPS and no sensitive data is transferred which satisfies the requirements for mitigate by design.

para.text: Code quality issues stem from failure to follow good coding practices and can lead to unpredictable behavior. These may include but are not limited to:

bulletitem.text: Neglecting to remove debug code or dead code
Improper resource management, such as using a pointer after it has been freed
Using the incorrect operator to compare objects
Failing to follow an API or framework specification
Using a language feature or API in an unintended manner

para.text: While code quality flaws are generally less severe than other categories and usually are not directly exploitable, they may serve as indicators that developers are not following practices that increase the reliability and security of an application. For an attacker, code quality issues may provide an opportunity to stress the application in unexpected ways.
The wide variance of code quality issues makes it impractical to generalize how these issues should be addressed. Refer to individual categories for specific recommendations.

text.text: The application fails to release (or incorrectly releases) a system resource before it is made available for re-use. This condition often occurs with resources such as database connections or file handles. Most unreleased resource issues result in general software reliability problems, but if an attacker can intentionally trigger a resource leak, it may be possible to launch a denial of service attack by depleting the resource pool.

flaw.description:
The program fails to release or incorrectly releases the variable 5__1, which was previously allocated by a call to system_runtime_dll.System.IO.StreamReader.!newinit_0_1().
Ensure that all code paths properly release this resource.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/404.html)

mitigation.description: Technique : M5 : Use industry-accepted security features instead of inventing your own Specifics : Using stream reader based on Microsoft C# documentation examples. Resource is managed by .net for shutdown Remaining Risk : none Verification : code and documentation reviewed

CWE-404 Proposal approved because Veracode does not accurately detect the usage of `using` blocks to dispose/release resources (https://community.veracode.com/s/question/0D53n00007dL1vA/404-improper-resource-shutdown-or-release-despite-using-using). Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design.

vulnerability.cve_summary: ajv is vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS). The vulnerability is due to passing attacker-controlled values from `$data` references directly into the JavaScript `RegExp` constructor without validation. This allowing malicious `regex` patterns that trigger catastrophic backtracking and cause excessive CPU consumption, leading to application-level Denial Of Service with a single crafted request.

mitigation.description: Technique: M5 : Use industry-accepted security features instead of inventing your own Specifics: These components/packages are 3rd party components used by other packages in our solution. As an additional layer of security, I have utilized NPMs "Overrides" section in our package.json to force a non vulnerable version of the packages when possible. Remaining Risk: Minimal to none Verification: Code reviewed and safety plan implemented for our next production release
